title: Why your lower back seizes when you feel financially threatened
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The checkbook and the spine

I was sitting at my kitchen table, staring at a credit card statement I didn't want to open. My jaw clenched. My shoulders crept up toward my ears. And then, like clockwork, my lower back seized. A sharp, gripping spasm that made me gasp and lean forward, my forehead pressing into the cold wood of the table.

This has happened to me more times than I can count. A bill arrives. A client's payment is late. I do the mental math of rent minus savings minus groceries and come up with a negative number. And my back, specifically the lumbar region, tightens into a knot that no stretch or ibuprofen can touch.

I used to think this was just stress. A normal reaction to financial pressure. Everyone gets a stiff back when money gets tight, right? But the more I learned about how the nervous system actually works, the more I realized something else was going on. Something deeper, more ancient, and far more specific.

Here's the thing. Your lower back is not just a mechanical structure. It's not simply a stack of vertebrae and discs that needs better ergonomics or a firmer mattress. Your lower back is a survival organ. And when your nervous system detects a threat to your ability to survive, it responds in ways that have nothing to do with posture and everything to do with protection.

Let me explain what I mean.

The money threat is a body threat

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, taught us something crucial. The nervous system doesn't distinguish between a saber-toothed tiger and an overdue electric bill. Not at the level of the autonomic nervous system. Your body reads threat through cues of safety and danger, not through intellectual understanding.

So when you look at your bank account and feel that drop in your stomach, your nervous system interprets that as a survival threat. Money, in our modern world, is directly tied to safety. Money means shelter. Money means food. Money means access to medical care. Money means you can keep your children safe. When that feels uncertain, your body activates the same ancient circuits that would have kept you alive on the savanna.

And here's where the lower back comes in. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades studying how animals and humans process threat. He observed that the lower back is a primary storage site for the freeze response. When an animal is trapped and cannot fight or flee, it freezes. The muscles tense, the body goes rigid, and the energy of the thwarted survival response gets locked into the tissues.

Your lower back is built to hold that tension. The psoas muscle, which connects your spine to your femur, is one of the deepest and most powerful muscles in your body. It's directly involved in the startle response. When you feel threatened, the psoas contracts. It shortens. It pulls your lumbar spine into a tighter curve. And if the threat doesn't resolve, if you can't fight or flee because you have to sit at your desk and pretend everything is fine, that tension stays.

Wild, right? Your back pain might not be about how you slept or how you lifted that box. It might be about a nervous system that has been trying to protect you from a threat it cannot escape.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). It walks through exactly how these threat responses show up in daily life and what to do about them.

The freeze response lives in your lower back

I remember a period in my twenties when I was barely making rent. I worked three jobs. I never had enough. And my lower back was in constant, low-grade spasm. I saw a chiropractor. I got massages. I did yoga. Nothing held. The relief would last a day, maybe two, and then the tightness would return.

I didn't understand then that my body was in a chronic freeze state. I was surviving, not living. And the freeze response, as Janina Fisher writes about in her work on trauma, is a state of immobility with high muscle tension. You're braced. You're ready. But you're not moving. You're stuck between the impulse to run and the reality that you have nowhere to go.

That tension collects in the lower back because it's the center of your body's movement potential. Your legs want to run. Your arms want to fight. But your core, your lumbar spine, is the hinge point. When both impulses are activated and neither can complete, the hinge locks up.

Think about it. When was the last time you felt financially threatened and actually ran? When was the last time you fought your bank statement? You didn't. You sat there. You stared. You worried. You maybe made a spreadsheet. But your body was screaming for action, and you gave it none. So it held the tension. It held it in your lower back.

This is not a metaphor. This is physiology. The psoas muscle is innervated by the lumbar plexus, which is directly connected to the sympathetic nervous system. When your sympathetic nervous system is activated, the psoas gets the signal to contract. And if you don't discharge that activation through movement, through shaking, through running, through some form of completion, the muscle stays shortened.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link), Peter Levine's book that explains exactly how this process works and how to release it.

How the nervous system gets stuck in financial fear

Here's what happens over time. The first time you experience a serious financial threat, your nervous system learns. It encodes that experience as a template. The next time you encounter anything that resembles that threat, your body responds before your conscious mind has even finished reading the email.

This is the gift and the curse of neuroception, the term Porges uses for how your nervous system scans for safety and danger below conscious awareness. Your body doesn't wait for you to decide if this is a real threat. It acts. And if you grew up in an environment where financial instability was chronic, where money was always a source of fear and conflict, then your nervous system may be calibrated to perceive financial threat everywhere.

Even when you're safe. Even when you have enough. Even when the numbers in your bank account are objectively fine, your body still responds as if the saber-toothed tiger is at the door. And your lower back still seizes.

I've worked with people who have six-figure savings accounts and still can't sleep the night before a quarterly tax payment. Their nervous system doesn't know the difference between a real threat and a perceived one. It just knows that money feels dangerous. And danger means the lower back tightens.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did your stomach clench when I mentioned that? Did your shoulders tighten? That's your nervous system responding to the idea of financial threat, even though you're just reading words on a screen. That's how powerful this conditioning is.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana, which offers specific exercises for calming the threat response and building ventral vagal safety.

What to do when your back seizes over money

I want to be honest with you. I'm not going to give you a five-step plan that will fix this overnight. That's not how the nervous system works. But I can tell you what has helped me, and what the research suggests, for beginning to unwind this pattern.

First, stop trying to stretch or crack your back when it seizes. This is counterintuitive, I know. But when the psoas is contracted in a freeze response, forcing it to lengthen can actually increase the tension. The muscle is trying to protect you. It's bracing for impact. Stretching it without addressing the underlying threat signal can feel like a violation to the nervous system.

Instead, try orienting. Look around the room. Find five things you can see. Three things you can hear. One thing you can feel. This is a polyvagal exercise that helps your nervous system register safety in the present moment. It tells your body, "We are not actually being chased. We are in a room. We are safe right now."

Second, move slowly. Don't jump into intense exercise. Gentle, pendular movements can help the psoas release. Lie on your back with your knees bent and let your knees gently fall to one side, then the other. Small. Slow. No forcing. Let the movement be a question, not a command.

Third, name the threat. Say it out loud. "I am afraid I won't have enough money to pay my rent." Saying it activates the prefrontal cortex, which can help down-regulate the amygdala. It brings the threat into conscious awareness, where you can actually evaluate it rather than just react to it.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Using it gently on the hips and lower back can help the psoas and surrounding muscles begin to unwind, especially when combined with slow breathing.

Fourth, and this is the hard one, you have to address the financial threat itself. Not just the body sensation, but the actual situation. Because if you are genuinely financially threatened, your body is responding appropriately. The nervous system is not wrong. It's trying to tell you something. You have to listen to that message and take practical steps to increase your financial safety. This might mean creating a budget, seeking financial advice, or having a difficult conversation about money with someone you trust.

I know that's not the easy answer. But the body doesn't lie. And if you keep ignoring the real threat while trying to relax your back, the tension will keep coming back. You have to address both levels. The body and the circumstance. The sensation and the situation.

The body holds the history

I don't believe in quick fixes for this. I believe in slow, patient, respectful work with the nervous system. I believe in learning to listen to what your body is telling you, even when it's uncomfortable. Especially when it's uncomfortable.

Your lower back seizing when you feel financially threatened is not a weakness. It's not a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that your body has been trying to protect you, in the only way it knows how, for a very long time. It's a sign that you have survived things. It's a sign that your nervous system is doing its job, even if that job feels like a prison sometimes.

The goal is not to never feel that tightness again. The goal is to learn to be with it. To understand what it's saying. To gradually, over time, show your nervous system that you are safe enough now. That you can handle the uncertainty. That you don't need to freeze every time a number changes on a screen.

This takes time. It takes patience. It takes a willingness to feel things you've been avoiding. But it's possible. I know because I'm still doing it. Some days my back is loose and free. Other days it seizes up the moment I open my email. But I understand now. And understanding, for me, has been the beginning of real change.

Sit with that. Let it land. And if your lower back is tight right now, just notice. Don't try to fix it. Just notice. That's enough for now.
